coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric detection of trace moisture in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol using N, P-codoped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuai
Fan, Xiaoyu
Jiang, Shuhan
Guan, Rentian
Shao, Xiaodong
Wang, Shuhao
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Trace moisture was firstly measured in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol at room temperature. The N, P-codoped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one pot solvothermal method, which displayed solvent-dependent fluorescence. The N, P-codoped CDs exhibited green fluorescence, with the quantum yields (QY) of 27.9%, 37.4%, 26.2% and 2.3% in methanol, ethanol, n-propanol and water, respectively. Based on the hydrogen bond interactions between the protic solvents and the N, P-codoped CDs, with the water content increased, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-codoped CDs gradually decreased in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol. The decrement in the fluorescence intensity is linearly related to the water content within the content range of 2% to 8%, 0.04% to 5% and 0.04% to 1.0%, with a limit of detection of 0.22%, 0.009% and 0.021% in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. Therefore, the present system has a prospective value for the application in the determination of trace water in the relevant fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
P-codoped CDs
Hydrogen bond interactions
Moisture

Detection of water in different solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and histogram of particle size distribution (A), FT-IR spectra (B), XRD spectrum (C), and XPS full spectrum (D) of CDs.
High-resolution XPS of C 1s (A), O 1s (B), N 1s (C) and P 1s (D) of CDs.
Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (A) and the normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in different solvents (B).
The (F0-F) value of samples in various organic solvents and inorganic salts. (organic solvents and water content=8%, the concentrations of the salts are 150 μM).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in different solvent containing various content of water (A, C and E), the corresponding linear relationship between (F0-F) and water content in different solvents (B, D and F).
Synthesis of N, P-codoped CDs and the photos of CDs under UV lamp in THF (A), acetone (B), DMF (C), ethanol (D), EG (E) and water (F).
Results for the detection of water content in real samples

head.ce:title: Fluorometric detection of trace moisture in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol using N, P-codoped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian, 710077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian, 710077, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace moisture was firstly measured in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol at room temperature. The N, P-codoped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one pot solvothermal method, which displayed solvent-dependent fluorescence. The N, P-codoped CDs exhibited green fluorescence, with the quantum yields (QY) of 27.9%, 37.4%, 26.2% and 2.3% in methanol, ethanol, n-propanol and water, respectively. Based on the hydrogen bond interactions between the protic solvents and the N, P-codoped CDs, with the water content increased, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-codoped CDs gradually decreased in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol. The decrement in the fluorescence intensity is linearly related to the water content within the content range of 2% to 8%, 0.04% to 5% and 0.04% to 1.0%, with a limit of detection of 0.22%, 0.009% and 0.021% in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. Therefore, the present system has a prospective value for the application in the determination of trace water in the relevant fields.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection and monitoring of water is important in chemistry and industry research, where water acts as the most common impurity in organic solvents [1]. For the typical organometallic reaction, in chemistry, water even trace amount can reduce the activity of organometallic compounds and lower the yield and even leading to the catastrophic failure [2]. Besides reactions, there are also some compounds facing easy hydrolysis during storage even in the presence of trace moisture [3]. In petroleum industry, the contamination of moisture in petroleum based fuels can cause fuel ducts clogging and even leading to engine damage and failure resulting from the emulsification and phase separation under low temperature [4]. In addition, it is also important to estimate moisture or water content in food and drinking samples [5].

There are numerous sensing methods available for the detection of humidity and moisture, which are based on various principles [6–9]. The most classic method for moisture detection in organic solvent is the Karl-Fisher titration, which is based on the simultaneous reaction of iodine and water with an alkyl sulphite reagent [10]. There are two typical techniques for moisture assay such as gas chromatography [11], and electrochemistry [12]. By comparison with the typical methods, fluorescence approach can show better sensitivity, selectivity, as well as simplicity and rapid response [13]. In this case, there are numerous fluorescent systems reported for water sensing. Most of these systems are based on various nanomaterials including organic frame works [14, 15], fluoranthene dyes [16], DMC and TMAPPZn [17], and carbon dots (CDs) [18]. However, by comparison with most of the above reported methods the CDs system for the detection of water in organic solvent has a simple process of synthesis and detection, a lower limit of detection (LOD).

CDs, as novel nanomaterials, have good aqueous solubility, excellent optical properties, low toxicity, simple synthesis, etc [19, 20]. So, they have been used in plenty of fields, such as sensing, solar cells, energy conversion, electronics, etc [21, 22]. Due to their unique properties, CDs have become an important material for sensing [23]. In this work, N, P-codoped CDs were prepared by one pot solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and phenylphosphinic acid, which can display solvent-dependent fluorescence. With the water content increase, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-codoped CDs gradually decreases in methanol, ethanol, and n-propanol. So, based on the hydrogen bond (HB) interactions between the protic solvents and the N, P-codoped CDs, they were successfully applied to the determination of water in methanol, ethanol, and n-propanol. Synthesis of CDs and their photos in various solvents under UV lamp including tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and water (Scheme 1 ).

Phenylphosphinic acid (C6H7O3P, purity≥99.5%) was ordered from Shanghai Perfemiker Material Technology Co., Ltd. FA, Acetone (C3H6O, purity≥99.5%) were purchased from Yantai Far Eastern Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Tetrahydrofuran (C4H8O, purity≥99.5%) and n-propanol (C3H8O, purity≥99.5%) were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Methanol (CH4O, purity≥99.5%), ethanol (C2H6O, purity≥99.8%) and o-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, purity≥99.5%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (HCON(CH3)2, purity≥99.5%), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, purity≥98%), CaCl2, FeCl3, ZnSO4, MgCl2, HgCl2, CuCl2, NaCl and KCl were procured from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment from Milli-Q water purified system.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM 2100 electron microscope. TEM worked with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, and a copper grid was used for CDs suspension. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using pressed KBr pellets on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the K-Alpha spectrometer. UV-vis absorption spectra were measured by an UH4150 spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were measured by an F-7000 spectrofluorometer. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a D/Max-2500 Diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 2θ ranging between 5° and 80°.

First, o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 0.072 g) and phenylphosphinic acid (0.15 g) were mixed in 20 mL of ethanol. Second, the mixture was introduced into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10 h and then cooled down to room temperature. The solid products were obtained in a vacuum at 60 °C and redispersed in 10 mL methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. Finally, the N, P-codoped CDs solutions were stored at 4°C for later analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 200 μL of CDs solution in methanol and 50 μL of water with different content were added to 1.5 mL of centrifuge tubes. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution and the blank were measured under 386 nm excitation for detection of water. water content in ethanol and n-propanol were as same as the analysis process in methanol. To examine the selectivity of this proposed method, the different materials (methanol, n-propanol, isopropanol (IPA), 1-Butanol, t-Butanol, DMF, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) were added into the CDs and detected, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructural character of CDs is shown in Fig. 1A. The dark spots observed indicate the formation of CDs. The average particle size of CDs is approximately 10.25 nm ranging from 7.25 to 11.75 nm (inset of Fig. 1 A). The structure of CDs and the functional groups on the surface were characterized with FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1B). The functional groups can be observed, including the stretching vibration of O-H, N–H, C=O, C-N, P=O, P-O-C and P-C at 3423 cm−1, 3244 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, 1423 cm−1, 1136 cm−1, 1043 cm−1 and 697 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of CDs has a broad peak in 24.6°, which indicates CDs in amorphous phase (Fig. 1C). The different atomic species and chemical environment of CDs were disclosed using the XPS. Fig. 1D shows four main peaks at 284.4, 399.0, 532.2 and 132.5 eV, which are related to C1s, N1s, O1s and P1s. Therefore, the CDs are consisted of C, N, O and P elements. As shown in Fig. 2 A, C1s owns two peaks at 284.2 eV (C-C/C=C) and 285.1 eV (C-N). Two peaks of O1s existed in 530.4 eV and 532.2 eV (Fig. 2B) represent O-H and O=C-O groups. The N1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2C) shows two peaks at 398.7 and 400.0 eV, owing to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N. The P1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2D) shows two peaks at 132.1 and 133.3 eV, corresponding to P-O and P=O.

There are two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 382 nm (Fig. 3 A), which are arisen out of the π–π* transitions of C=C and the n–π* transition of C=N, respectively. Meanwhile, CDs display the best emission at 521 nm with excitation at 386 nm. Fig. S1 (in supporting information) shows that the peak intensity has a difference with the change of excitation wavelength (366 nm-406 nm), but the fluorescence peak position dose not change obviously. The fluorescence gets to the maximum with excitation at 386 nm in methanol, ethanol, and n-propanol, respectively. The solvent effect on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs was studied in different solvents (THF, acetone, DMF, methanol, EG and water) (Fig. 3B), with increasing solvent polarity, the emission peak of CDs shows red shift from 516 nm to 556 nm. It can distinctly demonstrate the unique solvent-dependent emission of CDs. However, the QY of CDs in different solvents were investigated (Fig. S2, in supporting information) by a reference method using quinine sulfate (QS) as the reference reagent. QY of CDs were 27.9% in methanol, 37.4% in ethanol, 26.2% in n-propanol, 2.3% in water under excitation of 386 nm, respectively. The highest QY up to 37.4% was measured in ethanol, and the lowest QY of only 2.3% was measured in water. So, it is obvious that the QY of CDs in alcohol is higher than that in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: First, the selectivity of this sensing system was investigated in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. The common organic solvents with similar structures as ethanol and mental ions were studied. They include methanol, n-propanol, IPA, 1-Butanol, t-butanol, DMF, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+. The fluorescence intensity of CDs with all these species are shown in Fig. 4 A, B and Fig. S3 (in supporting information). The results display that the fluorescence intensity change of CDs hardly arises in these common organic solvents and metal ions. Based on this the present method designed has high selectivity toward water detection. For the impurity of solvent, trace amount of water cannot be removed from the solvent, so the trace water serves as background for the preparation of CDs and assay. For the analysis, 250 μL of CDs solution reacts with 1 μL of solvents in the experiment. As shown in Fig. S4 (in supporting information), moisture barely makes the fluorescence intensity of CDs change in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. So, the fluorescence change from the trace water in solvent can be ignored.

Second, water with the different contents in methanol was added to CDs system in order to determine the change of fluorescence intensity, which was shown in Fig. 5 A. It displays that the fluorescence intensity decrease gradually with the increase of water content. As shown in Fig. 5B, the difference between the initiative fluorescence value and the changing fluorescence value (F0-F) has a good linear relationship with water content ranging from 2% to 8%. The linear regression equation is F0-F = -136.60 [water] (%) + 214.93 (R2 = 0.993) with the water content (2%-8%). In this equation, (F0-F) and [water] mean the difference between fluorescence intensity of CDs+water and FL intensity of CDs, and water content, respectively. The LOD for water detection can be estimated to be 0.22%.

Water with content from 0.04% to 5% was detected to test the quantitative assay ability of CDs in ethanol (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with increasing water content. A good linear correlation is shown between the (F0-F) and the content of water in the range from 0.04% to 5% (Fig. 5D). The equation of linear regression is F0-F = 479.56 [water] (%) + 78.40, (R2 = 0.998) with the water content (0.04%-5%). The LOD can be estimated to be 0.009%.

Fig. 5E shows the quenching effect of the fluorescence intensity towards water in n-propanol. The fluorescence intensity gradually reduces with water content increasing (0.04%-1.0%), the plot of the fluorescence intensity of CDs via water content fits a linear equation (Fig. 5F). The (F0-F) decreases with the content of water from 0.04% to 1.0%. The (F0-F) intensity and content of water (0.04%-1.0%) exhibits a good linear relationship (F0-F = 1015.38 [water] (%) + 22.37, R2 = 0.998). The LOD can be estimated to be 0.021%.

As shown in Fig. S5, the lifetime of CDs was measured with and without water to be 10.61 ns, 13.79 ns in methanol, 10.93 ns, 7.98 ns in ethanol, and 11.71 ns and 9.30 ns in n-propanol, respectively. It is obvious that the average lifetime value of CDs reduces in the presence of water. The decrease in lifetime is owing to the fact that the quenching is an additional rate process. The excited state reduced in the absence of fluorescence emission. So, water caused the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The sensing mechanism of CDs is demonstrated (Fig. 3B), which depends on the solvent polarity. With increasing solvent polarity, the emission peak of CDs displays red shift. The solvent-dependence of CDs in protic solvents primarily attributes to HB interactions between the protic solvents and CDs [13, 18]. In the presence of pyridinic-N [24] (Fig. 2C), CDs have high electron density and belong to strong acceptor of HB. They can augment the possibility of nonradiative relaxation of CDs and bring about the descent of fluorescence intensity in the presence of HB donating molecules, such as methanol and water.

Methanol, ethanol and n-propanol spiked with different water contents were applied to the detection of real samples to estimate this method in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol. Table 1 shows the feasibility in real samples. The recoveries range from 100.5% to 105.1% for the determination of water content in methanol, 95% to 106.7% for the determination of water in ethanol and 97.5% to 108% for the detection of water in n-propanol, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.4%, 6.3% and 5.1%. Thus, this method may be applied to the determination of water in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol.

In summary, CDs were prepared as fluorescent probes for water detection. CDs exhibit green fluorescence, with the QY of 27.9%, 37.4%, 26.2% and 2.3% in methanol, ethanol, n-propanol and water. Due to the HB interactions between the protic solvents and the CDs, water can contribute to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Thus, it can serve as an easy way for water detection in organic solvents, and this method is hardly effected by metal ions and common organic solvents. It also has lower LOD, which are 0.22%, 0.009% and 0.021% in methanol, ethanol and n-propanol, respectively. Therefore, it has a prospective value for the determination of trace water in organic samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Natural Science Foundation of China (91543206), Graduate Education Quality Improvement Plan of Shandong Province (SDYJG21198), and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022 and 318012116).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2022.134186.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace water in organic solvents based on carbon quantum dots with yellow fluorescence
